content.text: When a bug lands in an AI product's queue, the triage engineer faces a question that does not arise for conventional software: is this a code defect, a model regression, a data contamination problem, or a guardrail failure? The answer determines who should own the fix, how urgently they must respond, and what remediation steps are even available. A severity-and-priority matrix tailored to AI products formalises these distinctions into a decision framework that the entire support organisation can apply consistently and quickly.
What It Is

A bug severity and priority matrix is a two-dimensional classification scheme that assigns incoming defects a severity level (how bad is the impact?) and a priority level (how urgent is the fix?). For conventional software, severity typically maps to user impact and priority maps to business urgency. For AI products, both axes must additionally account for failure modes unique to machine learning systems: model regressions that degrade output quality without throwing errors, guardrail bypasses that expose users to harmful content, hallucinations that generate plausible but false information, and data exposure incidents triggered by unintended prompt leakage.

The matrix is usually implemented as a 4x4 grid: four severity levels (SEV1 through SEV4) crossed with four priority levels (P1 through P4). Each cell contains a response SLA, the owning team or role, and any required escalation path. The matrix is a reference artifact that triage engineers consult during the first minutes of handling a new report, before detailed investigation begins.

Severity is determined by the scope and nature of impact. Priority is determined by business context: a low-severity cosmetic defect may be P1 if it affects a Fortune 100 customer's demo environment that is live in two hours. The two dimensions are independent, and the matrix encodes response obligations for every combination.
Why It Matters

AI systems fail in ways that are easy to underclassify. A guardrail bypass that affects a single user session looks, from a metrics standpoint, like a low-impact event. But if the bypass caused the model to return a user's confidential system prompt to another user, the actual severity is critical regardless of breadth. A matrix that includes explicit AI-specific criteria prevents the triage engineer from defaulting to conventional software severity heuristics that do not account for these failure types.

Consistency matters because triage decisions made under time pressure are the ones most prone to individual judgment variation. A matrix gives every member of the support team the same decision rules, which means that escalation thresholds, SLA clocks, and response team assignments are predictable across shifts, time zones, and experience levels.

The matrix also serves as a communication tool. When the support team tells an engineering team that a bug is classified SEV2/P1, everyone in the organisation understands what that means in terms of expected response time, escalation path, and remediation urgency. Without a shared vocabulary, cross-team coordination during an active incident degenerates into repeated negotiations about whose problem this is and how fast they need to move.
Benefits
Consistent triage across the team.
All engineers apply the same criteria, making severity and priority assignments predictable regardless of who performs the triage.
Explicit AI-specific criteria.
Guardrail bypass, model regression, and data exposure are first-class severity signals, not afterthoughts that must be mapped onto conventional software categories.
Clear SLA commitments.
Every cell in the matrix carries a response SLA that the support team can communicate to customers and measure against in retrospectives.
Defined escalation paths.
The matrix specifies which team or role owns each severity-priority combination, eliminating ad-hoc escalation negotiations during active incidents.
Auditability.
Recorded matrix assignments create a dataset of historical classifications that can be analysed to identify patterns, refine the matrix, and train new engineers.
Faster initial response.
With explicit criteria, the triage engineer spends less time deliberating and more time acting on the first-response obligation.
Trade-offs
Rigidity under novel failure modes.
A matrix built for known AI failure types may not map cleanly to a failure mode that has not been seen before. Triage engineers need authority to override the matrix for genuinely novel situations.
Criteria calibration effort.

AI-specific thresholds (what fraction of model regression score constitutes SEV2 versus SEV3?) require careful calibration against historical incidents and may need revisiting as the model and product evolve.
Priority inflation.

Stakeholders with business relationships to specific customers tend to lobby for higher priorities. Without disciplined enforcement of the matrix criteria, priority assignments drift upward, destroying the SLA signal.
False precision.
A numeric score for 'severity' implies more precision than the underlying qualitative assessment actually provides. Teams must treat the matrix as a decision aid, not a measurement instrument.
How It Works

Triage begins when a new report arrives, either from a customer-facing intake channel, an internal alert system, or an automated monitoring pipeline. The triage engineer collects the required signals from the reporter, the ticket system, and the AI system's observability tooling. They then apply the severity criteria to assign a SEV level, apply the priority criteria to assign a P level, look up the corresponding cell in the matrix to find the SLA and response team, and start the SLA clock immediately.

For AI-specific incidents, the triage engineer must also capture the AI context block: model version, prompt revision in effect at the time of the incident, any evaluation scores from recent runs of the regression test suite, and the version of any guardrail classifiers active at the time. This context is essential for the engineering team to reproduce and diagnose the issue and should be captured at triage time, not after escalation.

The triage engineer then assigns the ticket to the appropriate response owner (L1 support, L2 support, ML platform team, or war room depending on the matrix cell) and posts the first customer communication if one is required by the SLA. From this point, the response team owns the investigation, but the triage engineer remains responsible for updating severity and priority if new information changes the assessment.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the AI-aware triage decision flow (top half) and the severity-priority SLA matrix with response team assignments (bottom half).

attrs.alt: Bug triage decision flow and SEV x Priority SLA matrix for AI products

content.text: Severity Criteria for AI Products
The following criteria govern severity assignment. They extend conventional severity definitions with AI-specific signals.
Severity
Conventional Criteria
AI-Specific Additional Criteria
Example
SEV1 (Critical)
Service unavailable or data loss for all users
Guardrail bypass confirmed; confidential data exposed via prompt leakage; model serving completely offline
Model returns another user's system prompt after jailbreak
SEV2 (High)
Significant degradation affecting many users; no workaround
Eval score drops >15% vs baseline; >10% of requests produce hallucinations; harm classifier false negative rate spikes
RAG pipeline returns outdated documents causing factual errors in 12% of responses
SEV3 (Medium)
Partial degradation; workaround exists
Eval score drops 5-15%; isolated hallucinations; latency SLO breached for <5% of requests
Summarisation quality degraded for documents longer than 10k tokens
SEV4 (Low)
Cosmetic or minor; single user; feature request
Minor formatting regression; single-user hallucination with no safety implication; outdated knowledge base entry
Model occasionally omits trailing punctuation in bullet lists
Priority Criteria
Priority is assigned independently of severity based on business and operational context.
Priority
Criteria
P1 (Business Critical)
Affects enterprise SLA; executive escalation; public safety implication; regulatory obligation; customer contract at risk
P2 (High)
Customer is waiting; SLA clock running; workaround requires significant customer effort; impacting revenue-generating flow
P3 (Medium)
Customer is informed; workaround available; no immediate SLA obligation; next sprint acceptable
P4 (Low)
No customer impact; internal quality improvement; backlog item; no SLA clock
The Composite Priority Score Formula
When triage is automated, a numeric score provides a consistent input to the severity and priority assignment logic. The composite priority score S is defined as:

Where I is the impact sub-score (weighted sum of user fraction affected, revenue risk, and data exposure risk), A is the AI-risk sub-score (weighted sum of guardrail bypass signal, model regression score, and hallucination signal), and U is the urgency sub-score derived from SLA hours remaining. A production multiplier (1.0 for production, 0.6 for staging) is applied to the raw score. The final score S in [0, 1] maps to priority tiers: S >= 0.75 is P1, S >= 0.50 is P2, S >= 0.25 is P3, otherwise P4.

The weights reflect the relative importance of each signal class for AI products. AI-specific risk receives a higher weight (0.35) than in conventional software triage because a single guardrail bypass or model regression can have consequences disproportionate to the fraction of users affected.
How to Implement
Implement the triage logic as a Python module with a dataclass for the input signals and explicit functions for each assignment step. This makes the scoring formula testable and auditable.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
ai_triage.py

Computes a composite triage score for an incoming bug report and
suggests severity and priority assignments.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal


Severity = Literal["SEV1", "SEV2", "SEV3", "SEV4"]
Priority = Literal["P1", "P2", "P3", "P4"]


@dataclass
class BugReport:
    """Inputs gathered during initial triage intake."""
    # Impact signals (0.0-1.0 each)
    user_fraction_affected: float     # 0=single user, 1=all users
    revenue_risk_normalised: float    # 0=none, 1=total revenue at risk
    data_exposure_risk: float         # 0=none, 1=confirmed exposure
    # AI-specific signals (0.0-1.0 each)
    guardrail_bypassed: float         # 0=no, 1=confirmed bypass
    model_regression_score: float     # drop in eval score (0=none, 1=total)
    hallucination_confirmed: float    # 0=no, 1=confirmed factual error
    # Urgency signals
    sla_hours_remaining: float        # hours until customer SLA breach
    is_production: bool               # True if affecting production


def compute_priority_score(report: BugReport) -> float:
    """
    Composite priority score S in [0, 1].

    S = w_impact * Impact + w_ai * AI_risk + w_urgency * Urgency

    where each sub-score is a weighted sum of its component signals.
    Higher score => higher priority (lower P-number).
    """
    # Impact sub-score
    impact = (
        0.5 * report.user_fraction_affected
        + 0.3 * report.revenue_risk_normalised
        + 0.2 * report.data_exposure_risk
    )
    # AI-specific risk sub-score
    ai_risk = (
        0.4 * report.guardrail_bypassed
        + 0.35 * report.model_regression_score
        + 0.25 * report.hallucination_confirmed
    )
    # Urgency sub-score
    if report.sla_hours_remaining <= 0:
        urgency = 1.0
    elif report.sla_hours_remaining >= 24:
        urgency = 0.0
    else:
        urgency = 1.0 - (report.sla_hours_remaining / 24.0)

    production_multiplier = 1.0 if report.is_production else 0.6

    # Global weights
    w_impact, w_ai, w_urgency = 0.45, 0.35, 0.20
    raw = w_impact * impact + w_ai * ai_risk + w_urgency * urgency
    return min(1.0, raw * production_multiplier)


def assign_severity(report: BugReport) -> Severity:
    """Assign severity based on AI-aware rules."""
    if report.guardrail_bypassed >= 0.8 or report.data_exposure_risk >= 0.8:
        return "SEV1"
    if report.user_fraction_affected >= 0.5 or report.model_regression_score >= 0.3:
        return "SEV2" if report.is_production else "SEV3"
    if report.hallucination_confirmed >= 0.5 or report.user_fraction_affected >= 0.1:
        return "SEV3"
    return "SEV4"


def assign_priority(score: float) -> Priority:
    """Map composite score to priority tier."""
    if score >= 0.75:
        return "P1"
    if score >= 0.50:
        return "P2"
    if score >= 0.25:
        return "P3"
    return "P4"


SLA_HOURS: dict[tuple[Severity, Priority], float] = {
    ("SEV1", "P1"): 0.25,  ("SEV1", "P2"): 0.5,  ("SEV1", "P3"): 1.0,  ("SEV1", "P4"): 2.0,
    ("SEV2", "P1"): 1.0,  ("SEV2", "P2"): 2.0,  ("SEV2", "P3"): 4.0,  ("SEV2", "P4"): 8.0,
    ("SEV3", "P1"): 4.0,  ("SEV3", "P2"): 8.0,  ("SEV3", "P3"): 24.0, ("SEV3", "P4"): 48.0,
    ("SEV4", "P1"): 8.0,  ("SEV4", "P2"): 24.0, ("SEV4", "P3"): 72.0, ("SEV4", "P4"): float("inf"),
}


def triage(report: BugReport) -> dict:
    """Full triage result for a bug report."""
    score = compute_priority_score(report)
    sev = assign_severity(report)
    pri = assign_priority(score)
    sla = SLA_HOURS.get((sev, pri), float("inf"))
    return {
        "composite_score": round(score, 3),
        "severity": sev,
        "priority": pri,
        "sla_hours": sla,
        "response_owner": _response_owner(sev, pri),
    }


def _response_owner(sev: Severity, pri: Priority) -> str:
    if sev == "SEV1":
        return "war-room (all hands)"
    if sev == "SEV2" and pri in ("P1", "P2"):
        return "senior engineer + ML platform"
    if sev in ("SEV2", "SEV3") and pri in ("P3", "P4"):
        return "L2 support"
    return "L1 support"


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    report = BugReport(
        user_fraction_affected=0.02,
        revenue_risk_normalised=0.1,
        data_exposure_risk=0.0,
        guardrail_bypassed=0.95,  # confirmed bypass
        model_regression_score=0.0,
        hallucination_confirmed=0.0,
        sla_hours_remaining=3.0,
        is_production=True,
    )
    result = triage(report)
    print(result)
    # => {"composite_score": 0.353, "severity": "SEV1", "priority": "P2",
    #     "sla_hours": 0.5, "response_owner": "senior engineer + ML platform"}
The triage module is then integrated into a webhook handler that receives incoming reports from monitoring systems or the customer support platform.

# triage_webhook.py
"""
Receives incoming bug reports via webhook, computes triage,
creates tickets in Jira, and posts alerts to Slack.
"""
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from ai_triage import BugReport, triage

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/webhook/bug-report")
async def handle_report(request: Request) -> dict:
    payload = await request.json()
    signals = payload["signals"]
    report = BugReport(**signals)
    result = triage(report)

    # Create Jira ticket with triage result
    ticket_id = create_jira_ticket(
        title=payload["title"],
        severity=result["severity"],
        priority=result["priority"],
        ai_context=payload.get("ai_context", {}),
    )

    # Alert Slack for SEV1 or P1
    if result["severity"] == "SEV1" or result["priority"] == "P1":
        post_slack_alert(
            channel="#sev1-alerts",
            title=payload["title"],
            ticket_id=ticket_id,
            sla_hours=result["sla_hours"],
            owner=result["response_owner"],
        )

    return {"ticket_id": ticket_id, "triage": result}


def create_jira_ticket(title, severity, priority, ai_context):
    # Placeholder: call your Jira client here
    return f"AI-{hash(title) % 10000}"


def post_slack_alert(channel, title, ticket_id, sla_hours, owner):
    # Placeholder: call your Slack client here
    msg = f"[{ticket_id}] {title} | SLA: {sla_hours}h | Owner: {owner}"
    print(f"Posting to {channel}: {msg}")
Examples
The following JSON shows a complete triage payload for a guardrail bypass incident, including the AI context block that the triage webhook captures from the monitoring system.
Real-World Example

A legal technology company deployed an AI assistant that helped users draft contracts. The team used a conventional four-level severity matrix inherited from their legacy software support practice. When the AI system began occasionally generating contract clauses that referenced the wrong jurisdiction, the triage engineer classified the issue as SEV3 (partial degradation, workaround available by manually reviewing the output) with P2 priority.

The classification was technically defensible under the conventional matrix: only a subset of contract types were affected, and the company told customers to review AI outputs before using them. However, the impact of incorrect jurisdiction clauses in executed contracts was potentially significant, and the failure mode was a hallucination (the model was generating plausible-sounding but factually wrong legal references) that the conventional severity criteria did not have a category for.

After the incident escalated to a customer complaint involving an executed contract, the team added an explicit AI-specific criterion to their matrix: hallucination in any output with real-world legal, medical, or financial consequence is SEV2 minimum, regardless of the fraction of users affected. They also added a 'domain consequence multiplier' to their automated triage scoring: if the product domain is flagged as high-consequence (legal, medical, financial), all severity scores are shifted up one level before the final assignment.

The revised matrix has since reclassified similar hallucination incidents at SEV2/P1, triggering a dedicated engineer response and customer notification within two hours rather than the 24-48 hour timeline the previous classification had implied.
Common Mistakes
Collapsing severity and priority into one dimension.

Treating all SEV1 bugs as automatically P1 (and P1 as automatically SEV1) discards the independent information each axis carries. A cosmetic SEV4 issue affecting the CEO's demo environment is legitimately P1.
Omitting AI-specific severity criteria.
A matrix that lists only 'service unavailable' and 'data loss' as SEV1 conditions will systematically underclassify guardrail bypasses and model regressions.
Not capturing AI context at triage time.
Model version, prompt revision, and evaluation scores are ephemeral. If not captured at triage time, they may be unavailable when the engineering team begins investigation.
Priority inflation under customer pressure.
Escalating all customer-reported issues to P1 regardless of matrix criteria destroys the SLA signal and causes the response team to burn capacity on low-urgency issues.
Failing to re-triage when new information arrives.
An issue classified as SEV3 on initial report may need to be upgraded to SEV1 when investigation reveals a guardrail bypass. The matrix must be applied at re-triage points, not just on intake.
No defined SLA for the SLA response itself.
The matrix specifies the response SLA for the fix, but it must also specify how quickly the triage engineer must complete the triage. Without a triage SLA, the SLA clock does not start promptly.
Best Practices
Version the matrix.

Treat the severity and priority matrix as a versioned artifact. When criteria change, record the change, the reason, and the date. Apply the new version to new reports only, not retrospectively to historical ones.
Calibrate AI-specific thresholds against historical incidents.
The 15% eval score drop threshold for SEV2 should be derived from incidents where that level of regression caused measurable customer impact, not from first principles.
Automate the initial score computation.
Use the composite priority formula to generate a triage recommendation. The engineer reviews and overrides if necessary, but automation ensures signals are not missed under time pressure.
Add domain consequence modifiers.
Products operating in high-consequence domains (legal, medical, financial, safety-critical) should shift severity up by one level for any AI-specific failure mode.
Track triage-to-resolution delta.
Measure whether the initial severity and priority assignments were later revised, and by how much. A high revision rate indicates that the matrix criteria need refinement.
Include the matrix in the new engineer onboarding runbook.
Every triage engineer must understand not just the matrix structure but the reasoning behind each AI-specific criterion before handling their first on-call shift.
Publish SLAs to customers.
Where contractually appropriate, share the severity definitions and corresponding SLA commitments with customers so they understand what to expect when they report an issue.
Performance and Scalability

At low bug volumes (fewer than 50 active tickets), the matrix is primarily a reference document that triage engineers apply manually. At higher volumes, automation becomes necessary to maintain SLA compliance.

The automated triage webhook described above can process a new report in under 100ms when signal collection is synchronous. For high-volume environments where signal collection requires asynchronous calls to monitoring APIs, the triage computation is best decoupled from the signal collection: collect signals asynchronously, store them in a queue, and trigger the triage computation when the signal set is complete.

False-positive rate in automated severity assignment is the key quality metric. A high false-positive rate (too many reports classified as SEV1) overloads the war-room process. A high false-negative rate (too many actual SEV1 issues classified as lower) violates SLAs. Calibrate the composite score thresholds by running the automated classifier against a labelled historical dataset and measuring precision and recall at each severity boundary.

For large teams, the matrix must be accompanied by a routing table that maps (severity, priority, product area) triples to specific team queues, not just role labels. A SEV2/P1 in the guardrail subsystem should route to a different team than a SEV2/P1 in the retrieval pipeline, even if both carry the same response SLA.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Jira Service Management.
Supports custom severity and priority fields, automation rules for SLA clocks, and escalation paths. The AI context block can be stored as custom fields on the ticket.
Linear.
Increasingly popular for engineering-adjacent support workflows. Supports custom labels and triage workflows but has fewer native SLA features than Jira SM.
PagerDuty Event Intelligence.
Can apply machine-learning-based severity suggestions to incoming alerts, which can be combined with the domain-specific composite score for AI incidents.
Datadog Incident Management.
Integrated with observability signals, making it straightforward to auto-populate the AI context block (model version, latency percentiles, error rates) at incident creation time.
Opsgenie.
Alert routing and on-call management; can apply triage automation via alert policies that map signal combinations to severity levels.
LangSmith / Langfuse.
LLM observability platforms that capture model version, prompt revision, and evaluation scores in a queryable format, making AI context block population automatable.
Custom FastAPI / Webhook.
For teams that need full control over the triage logic, a lightweight webhook handler running the composite score model is the most flexible option.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
AI-Specific Criteria
SLA Definition
Automation Support
Calibration Effort
Best For
SEV/P matrix with AI extensions (this topic)
Explicit
Per cell
High (webhook + scoring)
Medium
Mid-to-large AI product teams
Conventional software SEV/P matrix
None
Per cell
High
Low
Teams where AI bugs are rare
Single severity axis (no priority)

Per severity
Medium
Low

Fully manual triage (no matrix)
Ad hoc
None

High-volume automated triage at scale
Security and Reliability

The triage matrix and the automated triage system handle sensitive information: customer names, user impact descriptions, model internals, and prompt contents. Access to the triage system and its data store should be restricted to support and engineering personnel, with audit logging for any access to AI context blocks that contain prompt or model artefacts.

The automated triage webhook must validate incoming payloads against a strict schema before processing them. An adversary who can inject arbitrary signals into the triage payload could cause the system to generate incorrect severity assignments, potentially suppressing a real SEV1 incident or flooding the war-room channel with false positives. Use authentication tokens and payload signing for all webhook sources.

The triage system must be available even when the product it supports is degraded. Host the triage service on infrastructure that is independent of the AI product's serving stack. A SEV1 incident that also takes down the triage tooling compounds the incident with operational blindness.

For incidents involving potential data exposure, the triage system must immediately trigger a privacy review alongside the standard response path. The matrix should include a flag for data exposure risk that, when set above a threshold, automatically notifies the data protection officer or privacy team in addition to the standard response team.
Mental Model

Think of the severity-priority matrix as a flight operations triage model. Air traffic controllers assess two independent dimensions for every aircraft: the nature of the emergency (severity - engine failure versus fuel low versus navigation issue) and the operational urgency (priority - runway occupancy, weather window, passenger count). The severity determines what type of response is needed; the priority determines how soon. A fuel-low alert (medium severity) on the only incoming flight to a remote airport with no alternate (high priority) demands the same response speed as an engine failure on a short-haul flight with multiple alternates available.

For AI bugs, the nature of the failure (model regression versus guardrail bypass versus hallucination) determines the severity. The business context (customer SLA, domain consequence, incident timing) determines the priority. Only by holding both dimensions independently can the triage engineer make a defensible assignment that the entire organisation can act on with confidence.
Key Takeaways
The bug severity and priority matrix uses two independent axes: severity (impact scope and failure type) and priority (business urgency), each from 1 to 4.
AI products require explicit severity criteria for guardrail bypass, model regression, hallucination, and data exposure that are not captured in conventional software severity definitions.
A composite priority score computed from weighted signals (user fraction affected, AI-specific risk, SLA urgency) provides consistent, automatable triage recommendations.
Each cell in the matrix specifies a response SLA, an owning role or team, and an escalation path; the matrix is a reference artifact, not just a categorisation scheme.
High-consequence domains (legal, medical, financial) should apply a domain consequence modifier that shifts AI-specific failures up by one severity level.
Automated triage via a webhook handler reduces manual scoring burden and ensures AI context (model version, prompt revision, evaluation scores) is captured at intake time.
The matrix must be treated as a versioned, living document that is calibrated against historical incidents and updated when new AI failure modes emerge.
